citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
The Attending Veterinarian (AV) was not informed of a left forearm injury to an adult, female De Brazza’s monkey, Lily, in
a timely manner. On August 13, 2025, the animal keeper’s notes for this animal show it was not using or bearing weight
on its left arm. The following day’s notes on August 14th show the animal was still not bearing weight, climbing with or
using to grab with her left arm. It also states she had lower energy than usual on this day. Veterinary records show that
the animal first received veterinary care Aug.15th with no apparent communication concerning the status of this animal
until then. Veterinary observation and treatment were continued, and the monkey was diagnosed with fractures of the left
ulna and radius which required surgical repair. The monkey was observed during inspection and continues to receive
veterinary observation and care. Fractures of bones are known to be a serious and painful condition, and this animal
would have experienced unrelieved pain, discomfort or distress due to failure to timely notify and convey animal health
problems to the attending veterinarian. Daily observation of all animals to assess their well-being, and direct and frequent
communication with the attending veterinarian is required so that timely and accurate information on problems of animal
health and well-being is conveyed to the attending veterinarian.
Correct from this day, 09/10/2025, forward.

The majority of the ground of the outdoor enclosure for 6 baboons was covered with areas of wet dirt or thick mud. There
were standing pools of water and thick, wet mud surrounding access to the only source of potable water. The animals
would have to step into this standing water to access their drinking source. During inspection the baboons were seen
walking through the mud without access to the den building. Improper drainage systems and standing water where the
animals are contained may result in noxious odors, spreading of diseases, insect infestation, and/or other health hazards.
Housing facilities for non-human primates must be constructed and maintained so that waste and water are rapidly
eliminated to keep the animals dry, minimize vermin and pest infestation, insects, odors, and disease hazards.
Correct from this day, 09/10/2025, forward.

An outdoor enclosure housing one leopard had at least 4 sharp, metal, nail tips protruding inward along the leopard’s
overhead walkway. Additionally, there were two protruding metal bolts on a wooden beam near the overhead walkway
access and one protruding bolt on a climbing log. Sharp pointed nails or protruding bolts in areas where animals may
contact them may cause animal injuries. Outdoor housing facilities must be structurally sound and must be maintained in
good repair to protect the animals from injury.

There were several areas of the facility’s perimeter fence which have been reduced in effective height. The tree nursery
next door has placed pallets of construction material and other miscellaneous items directly next to and against the
outside of the perimeter fence. In some sections the material was more than half the height or about the same height as
the top of the fence. Along the western perimeter fence line were several overgrown trees inside and adjacent the fence
that extended up and over which could allow for potential animal escape. Also, along the inner, western perimeter fence
was a pile of rocks and cut trees approximately 5 ft tall. Stored materials or items and overgrown trees next to the
perimeter fence reduces its effective height and may facilitate the entry of unauthorized humans or animals or facilitate
animal escape. The fence must be constructed so that it protects the animals in the facility by restricting animals and
unauthorized persons from going through or under it and having contact with the animals of the facility and so that it can
function as a secondary containment system for the animals in the facility.
Correct by 11/10/2025.

The enclosure with two Black-necked swans and two ducks did not provide access to a potable water source. The
enclosure contained a pool/bath for swimming, however there was no receptacle with potable water available. A fresh
source of potable water is necessary to ensure the health and well-being of the birds. A bowl of fresh water was placed in
the enclosure during inspection. The licensee must provide all the birds with easy and convenient access to clean and
potable water.
Corrected during the inspection.

Multiple enclosures housing several different species of birds had walls with exposed damaged plywood and/or
particleboard. Several wood feeding platforms in the Vulture enclosures were excessively worn or damaged. Many of the
parrots' enclosures had chewed and worn or damaged wooden nest boxes. A number of enclosures had perches located
near overhead misters which had left a coating of a green, thick, slimy material on the perch surface. All of these affected
surfaces are not able to be effectively cleaned and sanitized. Failure to maintain surfaces that are suitable for cleaning
and sanitization can increase the risk of exposing the birds to disease and organisms that can be harmful to their health
and well-being. Primary enclosures must be constructed and maintained so that all surfaces in contact with the birds may
be readily cleaned and/or sanitized in accordance with 3.158 or be replaced when worn or soiled.
Correct by 10/10/2025.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
Numerous wild birds have inhabited the facility including its ponds, moats, and several regulated bird enclosures. Of the
many wild birds, Mallard ducks were observed swimming in the free water ways of the facility including regulated
waterfowl enclosures. Peafowl were observed walking along the tops of regulated bird enclosures passing droppings
directly into the enclosure. In some enclosures, wild ducks significantly outnumbered the exhibited birds, and the wild
ducks and sparrows were observed competing with the regulated birds for food and water resources. The droppings of
wild birds were observed throughout multiple surfaces of the facility, including near or within regulated bird enclosures.
Failure to adequately manage avian pests or other pests may lead to the spread of disease or other health hazards,
contamination of food and water sources, and may cause stress or discomfort to the regulated bird species. A safe and
effective program for the control of all pests including avian and mammalian must be established and maintained so as to
promote the health and well-being of the birds and reduce contamination by pests in animal areas.
Correct by 10/10/2025.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
Additional Inspectors:
Geoffrey Olsen, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER
Gustavo Soberano, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
2.40(b)(3)  Critical
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
The Attending Veterinarian (AV) was not informed of a left forearm injury to an adult, female De Brazza’s monkey, Lily, in

Correct from this day, 09/10/2025, forward. 
3.75(f) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
The majority of the ground of the outdoor enclosure for 6 baboons was covered with areas of wet dirt or thick mud. There

eliminated to keep the animals dry, minimize vermin and pest infestation, insects, odors, and disease hazards.

3.125(a)  Repeat

Facilities, general. 
An outdoor enclosure housing one leopard had at least 4 sharp, metal, nail tips protruding inward along the leopard’s

There were several areas of the facility’s perimeter fence which have been reduced in effective height. The tree nursery

3.153(a)(1)(viii) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
The enclosure with two Black-necked swans and two ducks did not provide access to a potable water source. The

3.153(a)(1)(ix)

3.158(d)

Numerous wild birds have inhabited the facility including its ponds, moats, and several regulated bird enclosures. Of the
